table.evidence: Probe 'Supports NULL byte in headers' failed with request headers: {"Accept": "*/*", "Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate", "Connection": "keep-alive", "User-Agent": "schemathesis/4.14.2", "X-Schemathesis-Probe": "Supports NULL byte in headers", "X-Schemathesis-Probe-Null": "\u0000"}
table.suggested_mitigation: Sanitize and validate all incoming HTTP headers to prevent NULL byte injection and other header manipulation attacks.

table.evidence: other_info: In the absence of an explicitly specified caching lifetime directive in the response, a liberal lifetime heuristic of 1 year was assumed. This is permitted by rfc7234.
table.suggested_mitigation: Review if the cached content contains sensitive information. If so, configure appropriate caching headers to prevent caching or reduce lifetime.

table.evidence: parameter: x-content-type-options other_info: This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type. At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.
table.suggested_mitigation: Set the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' to prevent MIME sniffing.

table.vulnerability: A Client Error response code was returned by the server
table.evidence: evidence: 404
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure the application handles unexpected input gracefully and returns appropriate error codes.

table.evidence: evidence: DELETE
table.suggested_mitigation: Configure appropriate caching headers (e.g., Cache-Control) to mark content as storable if it's not sensitive and can benefit from caching.

table.evidence: parameter: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy description: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.

table.evidence: parameter: Sec-Fetch-Dest description: Specifies how and where the data would be used. For instance, if the value is audio, then the requested resource must be audio data and not any other type of resource.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Dest header is included in request headers.

table.evidence: parameter: Sec-Fetch-Mode description: Allows to differentiate between requests for navigating between HTML pages and requests for loading resources like images, audio etc.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Mode header is included in request headers.

table.evidence: parameter: Sec-Fetch-Site description: Specifies the relationship between request initiator's origin and target's origin.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Site header is included in request headers.

table.evidence: parameter: Sec-Fetch-User description: Specifies if a navigation request was initiated by a user.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-User header is included in user initiated requests.

table.vulnerability: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
table.evidence: description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, C...
table.suggested_mitigation: Implement a Content Security Policy (CSP) header to declare approved sources of content and mitigate XSS and data injection attacks.

table.evidence: parameter: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy description: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is a response header that prevents a document from loading any cross-origin resources that don't explicitly grant the document permission (using CORP or CORS).
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.

table.evidence: parameter: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy description: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is a response header that allows a site to control if others included documents share the same browsing context. Sharing the same browsing context with untrusted documents might lead to data leak.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.

table.evidence: parameter: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy description: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.

table.evidence: parameter: x-frame-options description: The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options.
table.suggested_mitigation: Implement either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options header to protect against ClickJacking attacks.

table.evidence: evidence: <script type="module" crossorigin src="/assets/index-CiF0xUcj.js"></script> other_info: No links have been found while there are scripts, which is an indication that this is a modern web application.
table.suggested_mitigation: This is an informational alert and so no changes are required.

table.evidence: description: Permissions Policy Header is an added layer of security that helps to restrict from unauthorized access or usage of browser/client features by web resources. This policy ensures the user privacy by limiting or specifying the features of the browsers can be used by the web resources. Permissions Policy provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to limit which features of browsers can be used by the page such as camera, microphone, location, full screen et...
table.suggested_mitigation: Implement a Permissions Policy Header to restrict unauthorized access or usage of browser/client features by web resources.

table.vulnerability: Server Leaks Version Information via "Server" HTTP Response Header Field
table.evidence: evidence: nginx/1.27.5
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

table.evidence: other_info: In the absence of an explicitly specified caching lifetime directive in the response, a liberal lifetime heuristic of 1 year was assumed. This is permitted by rfc7234.
table.suggested_mitigation: Review if the cached content contains sensitive information. If so, configure appropriate caching headers to prevent caching or reduce lifetime.

table.evidence: parameter: x-content-type-options other_info: This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type. At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.
table.suggested_mitigation: Set the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' to prevent MIME sniffing.

table.evidence: evidence: nginx/1.27.5 other_info: There is a chance that the highlight in the finding is on a value in the headers, versus the actual matched string in the response body.
table.suggested_mitigation: Configure the server to prevent information leaks, such as version banners.

table.evidence: parameter: Header User-Agent attack: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Trident/7.0; rv:11.0) like Gecko
table.suggested_mitigation: This is an informational finding from a fuzzer, no direct mitigation required unless specific vulnerabilities are identified.

notes: ZAP confidence levels (1, 2, 3) have been mapped to low, medium, high respectively.
Schemathesis findings' severity and confidence were inferred based on the nature of the reported issue (test failure for NULL byte injection).
Suggested mitigations for ZAP findings were extracted from the 'solution' field, or inferred from the 'description' if the solution was incomplete or generic.
Threat model relation is marked as 'unknown' for all findings as no specific threat model context was provided in the scanner outputs.
